name: Lou McGarity
abstract: Robert Louis McGarity (July 22, 1917 – August 28, 1971) was an American jazz trombonist who was a member of the Benny Goodman big band during the late 1930s and early 1940s. After serving in the military, he was a studio musician in New York City who performed in clubs at night with Eddie Condon and the Lawson/Haggard band.

concerts.label: Jazz: The Personal Dimension: Pee Wee Erwin All-Stars

works.t: I Thought I Heard Buddy Bolden Say

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Conniff
about_quotes.evidence: I was using the guys that were playing all those things out on the road in the days I was on the road. Like Billy Butterfield was playing lead trumpet, Lou McGarity was playing lead trombone...
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Conniff interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 14, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: George Masso
about_quotes.evidence: Lou was responsible for me playing trombone. I just heard his recording of “Yours” with the Benny Goodman Orchestra... I heard it and I said that’s what I want to play.
about_quotes.doc_title: George Masso interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 10, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Bud Freeman
about_quotes.evidence: I had Lou McGarity, I had some really good players and we did -- had a jazz background for all the singers that came out and then did some jazz things ourselves.

about_quotes.speaker: William Randolph "Cozy" Cole
about_quotes.evidence: Lou McGarity trombone, and so we did about 12 concerts, Battle of Swing between Louie and Benny Goodman.

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: Afterwards, he had Lou McGarity and Peanuts and myself over to his table and he said, 'Lucille, by my judge and witness; when I reorganize and I get to feel better, I want these three boys in my band'

about_quotes.speaker: Dave McKenna
about_quotes.evidence: I love those guys, Cutty and Lou McGarity, and Peanuts and Bob Wilber... At first we had Buck Clayton when I was at Condon’s and then Yank Lawson.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dave McKenna interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997
